content.text: WSGI and ASGI are the two
that define a contract between a Python web application and the server process that hosts it. Neither is a library or a framework — each is a
that any web server and any web framework can independently implement, making all compliant servers and frameworks interchangeable.
(Web Server Gateway Interface, PEP 3333) defines a synchronous, single-call protocol: a Python application is a callable that takes two arguments —
(a dictionary of CGI-style HTTP headers and metadata) and
(a callback for writing the HTTP status and headers) — and returns an iterable of response-body bytes. One call in, one response out: the call blocks the worker thread until the response is complete.
(Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface) supersedes WSGI for Python 3.5+ environments. An ASGI application is an
(an async callable that yields incoming events such as request body chunks or WebSocket messages), and
(an async callable for emitting response events). The ASGI model supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and long-lived connections such as WebSockets and Server-Sent Events over the same server process.

For agentic AI applications — which frequently serve streaming LLM responses, maintain persistent WebSocket channels to frontend UIs, or expose REST endpoints that orchestrate multi-step tool calls — ASGI is almost always the correct foundation.
Understanding WSGI and ASGI matters for two practical reasons. First,

— Flask, Django, FastAPI, Starlette, Quart — is built on top of one of these interfaces. Knowing the interface tells you which servers can run it, how it scales, and what communication patterns it supports. Second, the choice between WSGI and ASGI shapes what your agentic system
can do at the transport layer
: only ASGI applications can stream tokens as Server-Sent Events, maintain WebSocket connections, or handle many concurrent slow clients without proportionally many OS threads.

. A UI that shows an LLM thinking step-by-step needs a streaming HTTP response or a WebSocket. A multi-agent system where a coordinator periodically pushes status updates needs Server-Sent Events. None of these patterns fit naturally in WSGI — they require holding a worker thread open, which limits concurrency. ASGI handles them natively.
Any WSGI or ASGI framework runs on any compliant server. You can swap Gunicorn for uWSGI, or Uvicorn for Hypercorn, without touching application code.

The interface cleanly separates HTTP parsing, TLS termination, and worker management (the server's job) from route logic, response construction, and business rules (the application's job). Neither side needs to know the other's implementation details.

An ASGI server runs a single event loop per process and interleaves thousands of coroutines. A single Uvicorn worker can hold 10 000 idle WebSocket connections — something that would require 10 000 OS threads under a threaded WSGI model.
callable can be invoked repeatedly, emitting body chunks incrementally. This is the mechanism that makes LLM token-by-token streaming possible in frameworks like FastAPI.
), so a single ASGI server process handles all three connection types simultaneously.
ASGI defines startup and shutdown lifespan events that allow the application to acquire connection pools, load ML models, or warm up caches before the server starts accepting requests.
. A request comes in, a worker processes it top-to-bottom, the response goes out. You can use any blocking library — database drivers, file I/O, standard
HTTP calls — without async plumbing. ASGI requires that all I/O inside a request handler be non-blocking (
-able), or the entire event loop stalls while one coroutine waits on a blocking call. Accidentally calling a synchronous database driver inside an ASGI handler is a common and subtle performance bug.
ASGI requires understanding Python asyncio, coroutines, event loops, and async-safe libraries. Teams new to async Python often introduce blocking calls that silently destroy concurrency.
Not all Python libraries are async-safe. Many ORMs (SQLAlchemy classic, Django ORM pre-4.1) are synchronous; they must be wrapped in
adaptor to avoid blocking the loop.

WSGI scales by adding OS processes or threads. Under I/O-bound workloads with many concurrent connections (typical for an LLM proxy), this wastes memory and context-switching overhead per idle thread.
Async stack traces are less linear. Tools like
help surface slow coroutines and unawaited futures.
The WSGI server receives an HTTP request, parses it, and assembles an
dictionary (defined in PEP 3333) containing keys like
(a file-like object for reading the request body). It then calls the application:
to commit the status line and headers, then returns an iterable of byte strings as the body.
The ASGI server maintains an event loop. When a connection arrives, it calls
dictionary carries connection metadata (type, headers, path). The application
calls stream body chunks incrementally, and
can be called repeatedly on a WebSocket connection.

Between awaits, the event loop is free to service other connections. This cooperative multitasking — not OS-level preemption — is why a single-threaded ASGI server can hold tens of thousands of idle connections without proportionally consuming CPU.

: an HTTP client contacts a reverse proxy (Nginx), which forwards to a WSGI server (Gunicorn) that spawns a pool of worker processes. Each worker handles one request at a time, blocking on all I/O. Scaling means adding workers, each consuming its own memory footprint.

: the same reverse proxy forwards to an ASGI server (Uvicorn) running a single asyncio event loop per process. The event loop multiplexes all connections — HTTP, WebSocket, SSE — concurrently, suspending each coroutine at
points to service others. The connection boxes at the bottom represent concurrent logical connections, not OS threads.

when building agentic AI backends that stream LLM responses, expose WebSocket endpoints for real-time UIs, serve Server-Sent Events for progress updates, or handle many simultaneous long-polling clients. This covers almost all production agentic systems.

for simple REST APIs, admin interfaces, or batch-trigger endpoints where every request is short-lived, all I/O is synchronous, and streaming is not needed. Existing Django or Flask applications are already WSGI and should not be migrated without a concrete reason.
(Django 3.1+, run via Daphne or Uvicorn) when you need ASGI features (Django Channels, async views) but want to keep the Django ORM and admin.
when your handlers perform many awaitable I/O calls — calling async LLM SDKs, hitting async databases, or fanning out concurrent sub-requests. Wrapping async work in
inside a WSGI handler is an anti-pattern that blocks the worker.
Choose your interface: for new AI projects start with ASGI + FastAPI. For an existing Flask/Django codebase, stay on WSGI unless you specifically need async I/O or WebSockets.
Install the matching server. For ASGI:
Write a minimal app (see Examples below) and verify it runs locally:
For production ASGI deployments, run Uvicorn behind Gunicorn's process manager:
. Gunicorn restarts crashed workers while Uvicorn runs the async event loop inside each worker.
Place Nginx in front of both. Configure Nginx to proxy to the application server on an internal port, handle TLS, and serve static files directly.
in FastAPI with a generator that yields bytes, or use

# Minimal WSGI application — runs with: gunicorn wsgi_app:app
# pip install gunicorn

"""wsgi_app.py"""

def app(environ, start_response):
    """
    The WSGI callable.
    environ : dict — CGI-style HTTP metadata
    start_response : callable — commits status + headers
    Returns an iterable of bytes (the response body).
    """
    method = environ.get("REQUEST_METHOD", "GET")
    path   = environ.get("PATH_INFO", "/")

    if path == "/health" and method == "GET":
        body = b'{"status": "ok"}'
        status = "200 OK"
    else:
        body = b'{"error": "not found"}'
        status = "404 Not Found"

    headers = [
        ("Content-Type",   "application/json"),
        ("Content-Length", str(len(body))),
    ]
    start_response(status, headers)
    return [body]          # must be an iterable of byte strings

# Minimal raw ASGI application — runs with: uvicorn asgi_app:app
# pip install uvicorn

"""asgi_app.py"""
import json

async def app(scope, receive, send):
    """
    The ASGI triple:
    scope   : dict  — connection metadata ('type', 'path', 'headers', ...)
    receive : async callable — yields incoming events
    send    : async callable — emits outgoing events
    """
    assert scope["type"] == "http"

    # Consume request body (required even if we don't use it)
    event = await receive()
    assert event["type"] == "http.request"

    path = scope.get("path", "/")
    if path == "/health":
        body = json.dumps({"status": "ok"}).encode()
        status = 200
    else:
        body = json.dumps({"error": "not found"}).encode()
        status = 404

    await send({
        "type": "http.response.start",
        "status": status,
        "headers": [
            [b"content-type",   b"application/json"],
            [b"content-length", str(len(body)).encode()],
        ],
    })
    await send({
        "type": "http.response.body",
        "body": body,
        "more_body": False,   # signals end of response
    })

# Streaming LLM agent endpoint — token-by-token via Server-Sent Events
# pip install fastapi uvicorn anthropic sse-starlette

"""main.py"""
import anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "ok"}

@app.post("/agent/stream")
async def agent_stream(prompt: str):
    """
    Stream LLM tokens as newline-delimited Server-Sent Events.
    The ASGI layer keeps this coroutine alive across many send() calls,
    so the HTTP response body is delivered incrementally to the client.
    """

    async def generate():
        with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        ) as stream:
            for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
                # SSE format: "data: <chunk>\n\n"
                yield f"data: {text_chunk}\n\n"
        yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(
        generate(),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control":     "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable Nginx buffering
        },
    )

# Run: uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
# Production: gunicorn main:app -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker -w 4

Consider a production agentic AI backend that exposes three endpoints: a REST endpoint for one-shot agent runs, a streaming endpoint for real-time token delivery, and a WebSocket endpoint for interactive tool-calling sessions where the UI can send follow-up messages mid-stream. None of the last two patterns are possible in WSGI; the streaming endpoint would require holding a worker thread open for the entire LLM generation time, and the WebSocket endpoint cannot be implemented at all.
The deployment stack for this backend is: Nginx (TLS, static file serving, upstream load balancing) -> Gunicorn with

(process supervision, four worker processes) -> Uvicorn event loop inside each worker -> FastAPI application. Each Uvicorn event loop concurrently handles several hundred streaming SSE connections and dozens of persistent WebSocket sessions without spawning additional threads.

. When a developer accidentally calls a synchronous SQL driver inside a coroutine, event loop lag spikes from under 1 ms to hundreds of milliseconds — a reliable early indicator before latency SLOs are breached.
post office with a fixed number of clerks

. Each clerk handles one customer at a time: they take the envelope (request), disappear into the back room (process), and return with the reply. While they are in the back, the customer waits at the window and the clerk cannot serve anyone else. Adding more clerks (workers) costs proportionally more money (memory).

managing dozens of planes simultaneously. The controller (event loop) is never exclusively focused on one plane; they issue an instruction (send a message), then the plane executes it while the controller attends to another. They return to a plane when it reports in (awaitable I/O completes). No extra controllers are needed for more planes — the same one sequences the work. However, if the controller personally tried to fix a plane's engine (blocking I/O) instead of delegating, all other planes would be left unattended — the ASGI equivalent of calling a synchronous library from inside an async handler.
Calling Blocking I/O Inside an Async Handler
The single most common ASGI mistake is calling a synchronous blocking function inside a coroutine without offloading it to a thread. Examples:
on a large file. Each blocks the event loop for the duration of the call, freezing every other concurrent connection. Fix by replacing with an async equivalent (
, async SQLAlchemy) or by running it via
Running WSGI Applications on an ASGI Server Without an Adapter
Pointing Uvicorn directly at a Flask or plain-WSGI application (without the
) silently runs the WSGI app but wraps every request in a thread executor, negating the concurrency benefits and potentially introducing thread-safety issues.
Forgetting to Consume the Request Body

before sending the response, even if your handler does not read the request body. Some ASGI servers leave the connection in an inconsistent state if the body is never consumed, causing subsequent requests to fail.

workers) does not apply to ASGI the same way. For ASGI, one Uvicorn process per physical CPU core is typical, since each process already handles thousands of concurrent coroutines. Provisioning 16 ASGI workers on a 2-core machine wastes memory and saturates the GIL.
Not Setting Response Headers for SSE
(for Nginx) to prevent proxy buffering. Without these, Nginx accumulates chunks and delivers them in one burst at the end, defeating the streaming intent.
Default to ASGI for new AI projects.
Almost all AI backends eventually need streaming or WebSockets. Starting with ASGI avoids a painful migration later.
directly in production lacks automatic worker restart on crash. Gunicorn provides process supervision; Uvicorn provides the async event loop inside each worker.
Initialize expensive resources (database connection pools, LLM client objects, embedding model weights) in the ASGI
startup handler, not inside request handlers. This avoids per-request cold starts.
Instrument your ASGI server with Prometheus metrics. A rising event loop lag (above 10 ms) indicates blocking operations leaking into the async path.
that drives an ASGI app directly without a running server, enabling fast in-process integration tests for async FastAPI endpoints.
Set request timeouts and body size limits.
to prevent slow-client or large-body denial of service.

For I/O-bound workloads — which describes the majority of AI application backends, where the dominant cost is waiting for the LLM API, a vector database, or an external search service — ASGI provides dramatically better resource utilization than WSGI. Under a workload of 500 concurrent users each waiting 10 seconds for an LLM response, a WSGI server needs 500+ worker threads (at roughly 8 MB of stack each, that is around 4 GB of RAM just for thread stacks). An ASGI server manages the same 500 connections in a handful of event-loop-backed coroutines, consuming a fraction of the memory.

Both WSGI and ASGI applications scale horizontally by running multiple processes behind a load balancer. ASGI processes need fewer workers per pod, so more pods can be scheduled on the same node budget.
Python's GIL means CPU-heavy work (dense matrix operations, image processing) does not parallelize within a single Python process regardless of WSGI or ASGI. Offload such work to
, a Celery task queue, or a separate microservice.

Under a purely CPU-bound hello-world benchmark, the WSGI/ASGI difference is small — both are limited by the GIL. The difference becomes large only when handlers spend significant time waiting on I/O, which is typical of LLM proxy services.

A starting point for production: one Gunicorn-managed Uvicorn worker per physical CPU core. Profile under realistic load before adding workers; more workers may not improve throughput if I/O is the bottleneck.
The most widely used ASGI server. Pure Python, asyncio-based, supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, WebSockets, and Lifespan.
The mature WSGI process manager. When used with
, it manages ASGI worker processes, providing automatic restart on crash and graceful reload on deployment.

The dominant ASGI framework for AI and data-science APIs. Built on Starlette, adds automatic OpenAPI schema generation, Pydantic request/response validation, and dependency injection. Natively supports
The lightweight ASGI toolkit that FastAPI is built on. Use it directly when you want minimal dependencies or are building a custom framework component.
Extends Django to support ASGI, WebSockets, and long-running background tasks, while keeping the Django ORM, admin, and auth system.
An ASGI server with native HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support. Useful when clients need HTTP/2 multiplexing.
class for FastAPI that handles SSE reconnection, event IDs, and proper header setting automatically.
Allows writing integration tests that drive an ASGI application in-process without a running server, enabling fast, network-free testing of async FastAPI routes.

WSGI and ASGI are both Python-specific. For Python AI backends, ASGI is the modern default. Twisted and aiohttp predate the ASGI standard and have their own event-loop abstractions that are not ASGI-compatible without adaptation layers.

Neither WSGI nor ASGI handles TLS — that belongs to the reverse proxy. Always terminate TLS at Nginx or a load balancer and connect to the application server over a private network or Unix socket. The application server should
be exposed directly to the public internet.
to reject oversized uploads before they reach application code.
Long-lived connections (SSE, WebSockets) hold server-side resources. Set a sensible idle timeout — Uvicorn's
and explicit ping/pong for WebSockets — to release connections from dead clients.
to reject requests with forged Host headers, preventing host header injection attacks in multi-tenant deployments.
When the ASGI app is accessed from a browser frontend on a different origin, configure
in production when credentials are involved.
events let the application close database connections and cancel in-flight LLM requests cleanly before the process exits. Gunicorn's
gives workers time to finish in-flight responses before a forced kill.
Pin Uvicorn, Starlette, and FastAPI versions in
. ASGI middleware stacking depends on internal Starlette APIs that can shift between minor versions.
WSGI (PEP 3333) is the synchronous Python web interface: a callable receiving
, returning an iterable of bytes. One request per worker at a time.
ASGI supersedes WSGI for async Python: a coroutine receiving
. One event loop handles thousands of concurrent connections, WebSockets, and streaming responses.
For agentic AI backends: choose ASGI + FastAPI + Uvicorn. Streaming LLM responses, SSE progress feeds, and real-time WebSocket UIs all require ASGI.
In production, run Uvicorn inside Gunicorn workers (
) behind Nginx. This combines process supervision with async I/O efficiency.
The most common ASGI mistake is calling blocking I/O inside a coroutine. Replace synchronous libraries with async equivalents or offload to a thread pool via
Initialize expensive resources (LLM clients, DB pools, model weights) in the ASGI lifespan startup handler, not inside request handlers, to eliminate cold-start latency per request.
TLS, CORS, request size limits, and trusted host validation all belong at the middleware or reverse-proxy layer — not hardcoded in route handlers.
